title: Accusative Case — When Articles Change
description: This is where most apps confuse people. We'll make it crystal clear: only ONE article changes in Accusative. Just one.

lessons.title: What is the Accusative Case?
lessons.subtitle: Every language has it — including English. You already understand it.
lessons.hook: English has cases too — you just don't notice. 'He hit him.' Why 'him' and not 'he'? Because 'him' is the object — the thing receiving the action. German does the same thing, but it shows it by changing the article, not the pronoun. This is the Accusative case.
lessons.explain: In every German sentence, you need to identify two things: WHO is doing the action (the subject — Nominative case) and WHAT is receiving the action (the direct object — Accusative case). The subject takes der/die/das/ein/eine/ein. The direct object takes den/die/das/einen/eine/ein. Notice what's different: ONLY the masculine article changes. Der → den. Ein → einen. Everything else stays exactly the same.

examples.german: Ich habe einen Bruder und eine Schwester.
examples.english: I have a brother and a sister.

breakdown.role: Subject (Nominative) — I am doing the having

vocabulary.memoryHook: haben ALWAYS takes Accusative — the thing you 'have' is always a direct object
sehen takes Accusative — what you see is a direct object
kaufen takes Accusative — what you buy receives the buying action
brauchen takes Accusative — what you need is a direct object

exercises.prompt: In the sentence 'Ich sehe ___ Mann' — which article fills the blank?
exercises.hint: Mann is masculine. What happens to masculine articles in Accusative?
exercises.explanation: Mann (man) is masculine — base article is 'der'. In Accusative, masculine definite articles change: der → den. So: Ich sehe den Mann. This is the ONLY change in the definite article system for Accusative.

exercises.prompt: 'Ich kaufe ___ Zeitung' — Zeitung is feminine (die). What goes in the blank?
exercises.explanation: Zeitung is feminine. In Accusative, feminine articles DO NOT CHANGE. Die stays die. So: Ich kaufe die Zeitung. This is the crucial insight — only masculine changes. Everything else is identical to Nominative.

exercises.prompt: What is the ONE article that changes in Accusative?
exercises.explanation: This is the key insight of the Accusative case: ONLY masculine articles change. Der → den. Ein → einen. Feminine (die/eine) and neuter (das/ein) stay exactly the same. Memorize this once and you have Accusative.

exercises.prompt: Arrange these words into a correct German sentence:
exercises.explanation: German sentence structure: Subject (Ich) + Verb (habe) + Object (einen Bruder). Bruder is masculine, so in Accusative: einen (not ein).

exercises.prompt: Complete: 'Er hat ___ Hund und ___ Katze.' (Hund=dog/masculine, Katze=cat/feminine)
exercises.explanation: Hund is masculine → Accusative indefinite = einen. Katze is feminine → Accusative indefinite = eine (no change from Nominative). Result: Er hat einen Hund und eine Katze.
